title: Contribution to global mean surface temperature rise from agriculture and land use
subtitle: The global mean surface temperature change as a result of a country or region's cumulative emissions of carbon dioxide, methane, and nitrous oxide. This is for [land use](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) and agriculture only.
caption:
Contribution to global mean surface temperature rise from agriculture and land use

The global mean surface temperature change as a result of a country or region's cumulative emissions of carbon dioxide, methane, and nitrous oxide. This is for land use and agriculture only.

This choropleth world map displays the 2024 contribution to global mean surface temperature rise from agriculture and land use, measured in degrees Celsius. The legend ranges from 0 °C to 0.05 °C, with specific tick points at 0.001, 0.002, 0.005, 0.01, and 0.02 °C. The darkest red shades, indicating contributions near or above 0.05 °C, are visible in Brazil, the United States, and Russia. These three countries stand out as the most intense areas on the map. Surrounding these high-value regions, countries like Canada, China, and Australia appear in dark red shades that sit in the 0.02 °C to 0.05 °C range. In South America, Argentina and parts of the Amazon basin show mid-range red tones between 0.01 °C and 0.02 °C. Across Africa, most nations sit in lighter orange shades, generally falling between 0.001 °C and 0.005 °C, with a few exceptions like the Democratic Republic of the Congo appearing slightly darker. Europe shows a mix of light and mid-range shades, with no single country reaching the darkest bands. Greenland and Antarctica are marked with diagonal hatching, indicating no data. The visual pattern highlights that the largest contributions to temperature rise from this sector are concentrated in a few large countries in the Americas and Eurasia, while much of Africa and parts of Asia show lower contributions.
